description: Sacred, historic temple said to be visited by the Buddha with paintings by a renowned artist.
display_review:
A Sacred Treasure of Sri Lankan Buddhism
The Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara is one of Sri Lanka's most ancient and revered Buddhist temples, constantly filled with devoted worshippers and curious visitors from around the world. This sacred site is not just a place of worship—it's a living masterpiece of art and history.
What makes this temple truly extraordinary are the stunning murals and hand-painted artworks that adorn its walls. These paintings showcase the unique style of Sri Lankan Buddhist art, created by master artists whose work has drawn admirers from across the globe. The intricate detail, vibrant colors, and spiritual storytelling in these murals are unlike anything you'll find elsewhere. Art enthusiasts and historians travel from far and wide specifically to witness these remarkable creations.
The temple grounds are always bustling with activity, but there's something magical about visiting in the evening. As the sun sets, the temple comes alive with a serene beauty—the soft lighting, the sound of evening prayers, and the peaceful atmosphere create an unforgettable experience.
What makes Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara especially sacred is that it houses precious relics of Lord Buddha, enshrined within the temple's pagoda (dagoba). According to tradition, the Buddha himself visited this site during his third visit to Sri Lanka, making it one of the most significant pilgrimage sites for Buddhists.

Practical Tips for Visitors:
Don't forget to buy flowers to offer to the Buddha—there are plenty of flower vendors available near the parking area. The temple has ample parking space, but it's important to note: do not leave your valuables in the vehicle. It's highly recommended to carry your belongings with you or have someone stay with the vehicle for security.
Whether you're a devotee seeking spiritual solace or a traveler interested in culture and history, this temple offers a profound and beautiful experience. Highly recommended for anyone visiting Sri Lanka!

description: Historic Buddhist temple with a robust collection of artifacts & statues, plus vocational training.

I had one of the most emotional and meaningful experiences of my life at this temple. The moment I entered, the staff were incredibly kind and guided me patiently on how to pray at the sacred Bodhi tree and in front of the Buddha relics. They never asked for anything in return, everything they did was with sincerity and compassion.
While praying, I unexpectedly became emotional and even cried. The environment, the relics, and the peaceful atmosphere touched me deeply. Out of gratitude, I offered 200 rupees, but they never requested or hinted for any donation.
This temple is truly special. A place filled with honesty, warmth, and spiritual energy. Highly recommended for anyone seeking peace or a meaningful connection. Entrance fee at 500 rupee

description: Serene Buddhist temple on floating platforms featuring numerous Buddha statues & airy shrines.
display_review:
Nestled peacefully on the waters of Beira Lake, Seema Malaka Temple is one of Colombo’s most picturesque and spiritual landmarks. 🙏🌸

Designed by renowned architect Geoffrey Bawa, the temple floats gracefully on platforms connected by bridges, surrounded by calm waters and lush greenery 🌿💧

The serene setting, adorned with statues of Lord Buddha ✨, intricate carvings, and traditional architecture, creates a perfect blend of beauty and spirituality. It’s not just a temple, but also a place for meditation 🧘, peace, and reflection. Visitors are enchanted by the lake views, gentle breezes, and the golden glow of the sunset over the temple 🌅💛

A visit to Seema Malaka is truly a calming and unforgettable experience, offering harmony for the soul and a glimpse into Sri Lanka’s timeless Buddhist heritage 🌺🕊️

description: Big, stately museum with royal objects & other artifacts relating to Sri Lanka's history & culture.
display_review:
The Colombo National Museum is the largest and most important museum in Sri Lanka. It’s a great place to learn about the country’s history, culture, kings, and ancient kingdoms. The building itself is beautiful, with a classic white colonial design and peaceful garden outside.

What You Can See Inside ;
•	Royal treasures from the Kandyan Kingdom, including the crown and throne of the last king.
•	Ancient statues of Buddha and Hindu gods from Polonnaruwa, Anuradhapura, and other old cities.
•	Old weapons, masks, coins, and traditional tools that show how people lived long ago.
•	Paintings and carvings that tell stories from Sri Lanka’s past.

Experience ;
The museum is very calm and quiet. It takes about 1–2 hours to walk through slowly. The displays are well-organized, and most items have English descriptions.

Good for ;
Anyone who wants to understand Sri Lanka’s culture and history, including students, tourists, and locals.

Tips ;
•	Go in the morning to avoid crowds.
•	Photography is allowed in some areas but not everywhere.
•	There is a separate ticket for the Natural History Museum next door.

description: Completed in 1930, this neo-Baroque structure now houses the national Presidential Secretariat.
display_review:
The Old Parliament Building of Sri Lanka, located in Colombo, is an iconic structure with historical and architectural significance. Constructed during the British colonial era, the building was opened in 1930 to house the Legislative Council of Ceylon, which later evolved into the State Council of Ceylon. It served as the center of legislative activities during a critical period leading to Sri Lanka's independence in 1948.

Designed in neoclassical style, the building features grand columns and intricate detailing, reflecting the architectural trends of the early 20th century. Post-independence, the building continued to serve as the Parliament of Sri Lanka until 1982, when the new Parliament complex in Sri Jayawardenepura Kotte was inaugurated.

Today, the Old Parliament Building houses the Presidential Secretariat and remains a symbol of Sri Lanka's colonial and post-colonial legislative history, standing as a prominent landmark in Colombo.

description: Former 17th-century governor's residence now a museum featuring artifacts from Dutch colonial rule.
display_review:
Dutch Museum, Colombo – A Quiet Glimpse into History 🇱🇰

I visited the Dutch Museum in Colombo Fort during a city tour, and it was a fascinating experience. Tucked away in the busy Fort area, this hidden gem offers a peaceful escape into Sri Lanka’s colonial past.

The museum is housed in a beautifully preserved 17th-century Dutch mansion, complete with wooden staircases, antique furniture, and classic architecture that take you back in time. The exhibits feature maps, ceramics, weapons, and household items from the Dutch colonial era, each telling a part of Sri Lanka’s rich history.

Some renovation work is ongoing, but it doesn’t affect the visit much, it will be even better once completed. I particularly loved the quiet courtyard, surrounded by old verandas, perfect for a moment of reflection or a quick photo.

It’s not a large museum, but definitely worth a visit if you enjoy history and architecture. The entry fee is reasonable, and the staff are friendly and helpful. A lovely stop while exploring Colombo Fort.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Colombo, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️ Colombo High Court - Visitor Information

🔓 Entry Time: Visitors can enter the premises after 8.00 AM.

🧳 Bags: Large bags are not allowed — police officers will stop you from bringing them inside.

🚗 Parking: Parking is limited, so plan to arrive early.

📜 Case Notices:

Check the notice boards or digital screens near each High Court door for the day’s cases.

✅ Double-check the date — sometimes old notices are not replaced.

⚠️ Some digital screens may not work, so rely on the notice boards too.

🔄 Court Changes:

Court numbers may change if there is a change of judges.

🗂️ If you’re unsure where to go, visit the Registrar’s Office and ask.

⏰ Start Times:

Court start times can vary — your summons will mention the time you should appear (e.g., 8.00 AM, 9.00 AM, 1.00 PM).

🕣 Some courts may start at 8.30 AM, depending on the judge.

📵 Phones: Keep your mobile phone silent or powered off inside courtrooms.

🪑 Inside the Courtroom:

Find the correct side to sit:

👮‍♂️ One side is for the complaining party (police officers, expert witnesses).

⚖️ The other side is for the accused party.

☕ Canteen: There is an on-site canteen with separate seating:

⚖️ One side for lawyers.

👥 The other side for the general public.

🚻 Washrooms: There is only one single-occupancy washroom available for visitors.

✅ Tip: Be early, bring only essentials, keep calm, and ask court staff or police officers if you’re unsure where to go.

description: An ancient and sacred Buddhist temple in Gampaha, reflecting Sri Lanka's deep religious heritage.
display_review:
🛕 Warana Raja Maha Vihara – A Hidden Gem of Spiritual and Historical Significance
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

Nestled in the peaceful village of Thihariya, just a short drive from the Colombo–Kandy highway, Warana Raja Maha Vihara is a truly remarkable destination for those seeking spiritual serenity, cultural depth, and natural beauty. This ancient Buddhist temple, believed to have been founded during the reign of King Devanampiyatissa (3rd century BC), is one of the oldest cave temples in Sri Lanka and a protected archaeological site.

🏞️ What to See
The temple is built across three levels on a rocky hill:
Pahala Maluwa (Lower Terrace): Houses the Sangawasaya (monk residence) and Dharma Hall.

Meda Maluwa (Middle Terrace): Features a drip-ledged cave temple, an 800-year-old stupa, and beautiful Buddha statues and murals.

Ihala Maluwa (Upper Terrace): Offers another stupa, a cave temple, and panoramic views of the surrounding paddy fields and hills.

You’ll also find:
Ancient Brahmi inscriptions
Lotus-themed cave ceilings
A reclining Buddha statue
Rock pools with fish
Tranquil forest paths and meditation caves

🧘 Spiritual & Historical Value
This temple is not just a place of worship—it’s a living heritage site. It served as a meditation retreat for monks and even as a refuge for King Valagamba during invasions. The temple has been renovated by several kings, including Nissanka Malla and Parakramabahu VI, who contributed to its artistic and architectural richness.

🧼 Cleanliness & Respect
The temple grounds are exceptionally clean and well-maintained. A heartfelt hat off to the temple caretakers and cleaning staff for preserving this sacred space with such dedication. Visitors are kindly reminded to:
Dress modestly and respectfully.
Dispose of waste properly using the bins provided.
Maintain silence and reverence throughout the temple premises.

🛍️ Local Flavor on Poya Days
On Poya days, a few temporary village-run stalls appear near the Bodhi Maluwa (upper rock terrace) offering:
Pol roti, wade, samosa
Fresh fruit juices, kadala, achcharu, and other local snacks
These stalls add a warm, community feel to the temple visit and are a great way to support local villagers while enjoying authentic Sri Lankan flavors.

🧘‍♂️ Facilities
Washrooms: Available and clean
Handwashing areas: Conveniently placed
Parking: Available near the entrance

📍 Location: Warana, Thihariya, Gampaha District
🕒 Best Time to Visit: Early morning or late afternoon for a serene experience and cooler weather

📸 I’ve included photos from my visit to help future visitors appreciate the beauty and tranquility of this sacred site. Whether you're a pilgrim, a history enthusiast, or simply seeking peace, Warana Raja Maha Vihara is a place that will leave a lasting impression.

description: An ancient and sacred Buddhist temple in Gampaha, reflecting Sri Lanka's deep religious heritage.
display_review:
Gampaha Asgiriya Raja Maha Viharaya is an ancient cave temple lying on top of a hillock on the Gampaha Minuwangoda Road. According to legends the history of the temple goes back as far as King Walagamba ( 89-77 BC) who is said to have used the caves to hide during his exile after 104 BC. The king is said to have built hundreds of cave temples all over the country at each place where he used to hide during the recruitment of a new army to defeat the invaders.

The main ancient temple lies at the top of the Asgiriya hill.  This has been used as a forest hermitage in the ancient times.  At a later date, this temple received patronage from a regional king called Sakala Wallabha during the time of  Parakramabahu the 6th of Kotte Kingdom in the 16th century.  The cave with drip ledges has been converted into an image house which is covered with paintings belonging to the Kandyan Era.
There is a bolder near the Bodhi Tree with inscriptions which has been protected by the Department of Archeology. This bolder also has a carving of the head of a horse.
A carving of a Sri Pathula (footprint of Buddha) can be seen on one of the rocks. An ancient pond now abandoned lies at the top. None of which has been dated.
It is believed that the name Asgiri was coined due to a horse of Prince Uttiya, the brother of King Kelanitissa being mysteriously lost on these hills.

About 100 years ago the priests had built an Alms Hall at the bottom of the hill since the benefactors offering alms were finding it difficult to climb to the top of the hill every day. Later a Buddha Image Hall and a Stupa were also built near this hall. A Bo Tree has also been planted here creating another complete temple at the bottom of the hill.

The temple is also known for a secret tunnel on the hill. This tunnel can be reached by stepping to the upper temple and turning right halfway. The access is through the jungle with thorny bushes. This path will lead you to a wide rock cave with a drip ledge. The cave becomes narrower as you walk into the cave. At the end of the cave lies the entrance to the tunnel. To reach the tunnel you would need to crawl between the rock and the floor. The cave entrance is now carved in cannot be accessed.

It is believed that there is a vast treasure hidden inside the tunnel and some believe that this tunnel was a secret route to pilikuththuwa rajamaha viharaya which lies about 8 km away.

There is also a deep hole on the rock next to the Stupa at the top. People believe that a treasure had been hidden inside this rock and covered with another stone and treasure hunters have destroyed the cover and plundered the valuables. Today only a hole covered by sandbags remains.

description: Sacred, historic temple said to be visited by the Buddha with paintings by a renowned artist.
display_review:
A Sacred Treasure of Sri Lankan Buddhism
The Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara is one of Sri Lanka's most ancient and revered Buddhist temples, constantly filled with devoted worshippers and curious visitors from around the world. This sacred site is not just a place of worship—it's a living masterpiece of art and history.
What makes this temple truly extraordinary are the stunning murals and hand-painted artworks that adorn its walls. These paintings showcase the unique style of Sri Lankan Buddhist art, created by master artists whose work has drawn admirers from across the globe. The intricate detail, vibrant colors, and spiritual storytelling in these murals are unlike anything you'll find elsewhere. Art enthusiasts and historians travel from far and wide specifically to witness these remarkable creations.
The temple grounds are always bustling with activity, but there's something magical about visiting in the evening. As the sun sets, the temple comes alive with a serene beauty—the soft lighting, the sound of evening prayers, and the peaceful atmosphere create an unforgettable experience.
What makes Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara especially sacred is that it houses precious relics of Lord Buddha, enshrined within the temple's pagoda (dagoba). According to tradition, the Buddha himself visited this site during his third visit to Sri Lanka, making it one of the most significant pilgrimage sites for Buddhists.

Practical Tips for Visitors:
Don't forget to buy flowers to offer to the Buddha—there are plenty of flower vendors available near the parking area. The temple has ample parking space, but it's important to note: do not leave your valuables in the vehicle. It's highly recommended to carry your belongings with you or have someone stay with the vehicle for security.
Whether you're a devotee seeking spiritual solace or a traveler interested in culture and history, this temple offers a profound and beautiful experience. Highly recommended for anyone visiting Sri Lanka!

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Gampaha, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
🕰️ Divulapitiya Clock Tower – A Forgotten Landmark

On a recent visit to Divulapitiya, I passed by the main junction where the Divulapitiya Clock Tower stands tall — or at least tries to. Once a symbol of structure and time for the community, today it looks tarnished, aged, and sadly not functional.

Positioned right at the heart of the town, the tower holds potential to be both a functional utility and a charming local landmark, especially for travelers passing through or locals navigating the area. But with the clock frozen in time and the structure weathered, it feels more like a forgotten memory than a town centerpiece.

🧞‍♂️ A Message to Local Authorities:
Public landmarks matter. They don’t just tell time — they tell stories.
The Divulapitiya Clock Tower deserves a restoration that brings back its dignity, charm, and usefulness. A small investment in repairs and cleaning could revive community pride and benefit daily commuters.

Let’s not wait until it disappears into neglect — let's restore what stands as a symbol of time and town identity.

#DivulapitiyaClockTower #GenieReview #PreserveOurLandmarks 🕰️🌱

It is famous for being one of the world's few hollow Buddhist stupas, allowing visitors to walk inside its vast dome. The site is a highly venerated place of worship, located directly opposite the sacred Kalutara Bodhiya (Bo Tree). The Bo tree is believed to be a direct sapling of the Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi in Anuradhapura, which itself originated from the tree under which Lord Buddha attained enlightenment. The stupa and Bo tree create a powerful spiritual landmark at the entrance to Kalutara. Many travelers stop here to seek blessings for a safe journey. A must-see landmark in the region.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kalutara, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Kande Viharaya is one of the most peaceful and spiritually rich places you can visit on Sri Lanka’s southwest coast. Located just a few minutes from Bentota and Aluthgama, this beautiful temple sits on a small hill surrounded by lush greenery — the name literally means “Temple on the Hill.”

The first thing you’ll notice is the towering Buddha statue, one of the tallest sitting Buddha statues in Asia. It’s an incredible sight, visible even from the main road, and looks especially breathtaking during sunrise or sunset. Inside the temple grounds, you’ll find beautifully detailed murals, ancient carvings, and quiet spots for reflection. The atmosphere is calm and welcoming, even if you’re not Buddhist — everyone is encouraged to explore respectfully.

The temple is well maintained, and the locals are kind and helpful if you need guidance or wish to make an offering. It’s a great place to learn a bit about Sri Lankan culture and Buddhist history while also enjoying some amazing views of the surrounding area.

If you’re visiting Bentota, Kande Viharaya is a must-see — peaceful, scenic, and spiritually uplifting. Go early in the morning or late afternoon to avoid the heat and experience the tranquility at its best.

Lankathilaka Maha Viharaya is one of the most beautiful Buddhist temples in Sri Lanka, located in Pilimathalawa, near Kandy. It was built in the 14th century during the reign of King Bhuvanekabahu IV of the Gampola Kingdom and stands majestically on top of a massive rock.

Recommendations:
Although the main entrance commonly used today is from the back, the old entrance is far more interesting. You will need to climb the ancient rock-cut steps, and you can also see the newly carved steps running alongside them. This old entrance is also accessible by vehicles, Take the route from the Lankathilaka M.V. (Lankathilaka Maha Vidyalaya) side. The road runs beautifully through the middle of the paddy fields.

Inside the temple, the main doorway is a massive wooden door, which is opened only once a year. For daily visitors, a smaller door built within the large one is used.

From the top, you get an almost 360 degree view at least a 300 degree panoramic view of the surroundings. If you look carefully from the stupa side, you can even spot the Ranawana Buddha statue in the distance.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kalutara, Sri Lanka.
A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kalutara, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
* Tranquil Location: Situated on the banks of the Kalu Ganga River, near the Dombagaskanda hill, the hermitage is surrounded by a lush 347-hectare wet zone rainforest. This natural canopy provides a peaceful and secluded environment ideal for meditation and spiritual practice.

* History: The hermitage was established in 1955, coinciding with the Buddha Jayanthi celebrations. Its founder is believed to be Olaboduwe Sri Revatha Dhamma Kitti Thera, who was also the principal of the Dharmadeepa Vipassana Pirivena in Kaluwamodara.

* Purpose: Bodhinagala Forest Hermitage serves as a retreat for Buddhist monks dedicated to meditation. However, it also welcomes visitors seeking tranquility and an opportunity to connect with nature and Buddhist teachings.

* Features: The hermitage includes kutis (small huts) for the monks, meditation pathways, and medical halls. A unique feature is the Gaudiya, a short tree trunk used as a bell to call monks to the main dining hall.
* Biodiversity: The surrounding forest reserve is home to diverse flora and fauna, including various bird species like the endemic Grey Hornbill, Rose-ringed Parakeets, and Hill Mynahs.

* Accessibility: It's reachable via the Panadura-Ratnapura highway (A8). From the highway, a short drive (about 1.5 km) along a minor road leads towards the Kalu Ganga and then another kilometer to the base of Dombagaskanda hill. While vehicles can go up, walking is recommended to enjoy the scenic forest paths.
In essence, Bodingala Forest Hermitage offers a tranquil escape into nature combined with a place of spiritual significance.

description: Built circa 1634, this palace was home to the monarch of the Kingdom of Kandy until the early 1800s.
display_review:
The Old Royal Palace, located within the precincts of Sri Dalada Maligawa (Temple of the Tooth Relic) in Kandy, is a fascinating historical site that offers a glimpse into Sri Lanka’s royal past. The palace, which once served as the residence of the Kandyan kings, is an integral part of the temple complex and holds significant cultural and historical value.

The Old Royal Palace was originally built in the 16th century during the reign of King Vimaladharmasuriya I. It was constructed to accommodate the king and his court, reflecting the grandeur and elegance of the Kandyan era. The palace complex was strategically positioned to be close to the Temple of the Tooth Relic, symbolizing the close relationship between the monarchy and the Buddhist faith.

description: An ancient and sacred Buddhist temple in Kandy, reflecting Sri Lanka's deep religious heritage.
display_review:
Gadaladeniya Rajamaha Viharaya with an ancient monastery is found on the flat rock at Diggala in the district of Kandy. According to an inscription, this temple was built by King Buwanekabahu IV in the year 1344. The chief architect of this temple has been a South Indian called Ganesvarachari. Therefore this temple is essentially built following the Indian Vijayanagar architectural style.

The vihara complex in Gadaladeniya is built over a rock plateau spreading from north to south. According to the Gadaladeniya inscription by Dharmakirti, the Devala at Gadaladeniya had been constructed for the protection of the vihara.

The main shrine room has a seated (in Vajrasana) Buddha Statue under a Makara Thorana and four standing Buddha images. The makara thorana is decorated on both faces with gods such as Brahma, Suyama, Santhusuta, Natha and Maithree, and two attendants.

The chamber within the Sikhara (dome) has had a Buddha Image but has been destroyed by the Portuguese. Next to the shrine room is a Devale (shrine) dedicated to God Visnu. According to Mahavansa, The great chronicle of Sri Lanka, It was “Upulvan Deiyo” thus Visnu who was selected as the guardian to protect the land of Sri Lanka and Buddhism within it at the time of Buddha’s passing away. Therefore this deity has a special place among the Sri Lankans.

By the side of the Visnu Devale is a Stupa made of a rock. This is believed to have been constructed by King Parakramabahu V. This stupa has a roof built over it held on 4 pillars. 4 smaller satellite stupas are built on small shrine rooms around the main stupa. This structure is called the Vijayothpaya or Vijayantha Prasada named after the mythical palace of god Indra.

This temple was neglected for some time and then it was handed over to the Velivita Saranankara Thero by King Viraparakrama Narendrasinha (1707 – 1739) and this temple has been looked after ever since by his pupillary succession.

Though this temple is known as “Gadaladeniya” today, An inscription identifies this temple as “Dharma Kirthi Viharaya” which is the name of the founding monk. The work “Nikaya Sangrahaya” identifies this temple as “Saddharmathilake” and “Saddharmalankaraya” identifies this as “Gadaladeniya Viharaya” after the village in which it is located.

description: Hindu temple built between the 14th & 15th centuries, featuring ornately carved wooden pillars.
display_review:
What is ambakke
"Ambakke" most likely refers to Embekke Devalaya (also spelled Ambekka Devalaya), a historical temple complex in Sri Lanka famous for its exquisite wood carvings.

It is a significant site for Sri Lankan art and architecture, located in the Udunuwara area of the Kandy District.

Here are the key details about Embekke Devalaya:

Famous for Wood Carvings: The temple is renowned for having some of the finest wood carvings in Sri Lanka and possibly the world, especially in the Drummers' Hall (Hevisi Mandapaya).

Historical Period: It was primarily built during the Gampola Era (14th century), attributed to King Vikramabahu III.

Deity: It is dedicated to the Kataragama deviyo (also identified with the Hindu God Skanda or Murugan), a prominent guardian deity in Sri Lanka.

Architecture: The Drummers' Hall is a marvel of ancient carpentry, featuring ornate wooden pillars and a unique structural element called the Madol Kurupuwa, a single wooden pin that holds 26 rafters of the roof together.

Carving Motifs: The carvings depict a huge variety of subjects, including:

Mythical creatures (like the entwined swans, double-headed eagles, and the elephant-bull hybrid).

Human figures (dancers, wrestlers, soldiers on horseback, and a mother nursing a child).

Decorative patterns (floral and geometric designs).

Would you like to know more about the specific wood carvings or perhaps its location and visiting information?

It was interesting to see how peaceful it was to visit in this quiet environment, which is perfect for meditation or contemplation. I highly recommend to visitors that they should respect the sacredness of the site; don’t make noise, and help keep the temple beautiful by helping to keep it clean. More likely than not, you will be the only visitor and you can walk around the grounds reading the tombstones and discovering what life might have been like for the 195 people buried there.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kandy, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Rating: ★★★☆☆ (3/5)

I recently visited the Asgiri Maha Viharaya in Kandy, and overall, it was an interesting and peaceful experience, though there were some aspects that could be improved.

The temple offers a beautiful and authentic insight into Sri Lanka’s Buddhist traditions. It’s one of the two main monastic orders in Kandy and has significant historical importance. The architecture is traditional and serene, and the quiet atmosphere gives you a moment to reflect and observe. The surroundings are quite peaceful compared to some of the more crowded tourist spots in the area. You can sense the spiritual energy and see how the monks live and practice their faith.

However, I would say the experience felt a bit limited for visitors. There wasn’t much information available in English, so unless you come with a guide or do prior research, it’s hard to understand the full historical or religious context. Also, while the place is definitely authentic, it isn’t as well-maintained or welcoming as other temples we visited in Sri Lanka.

It’s not a typical tourist attraction – and that’s both a strength and a weakness. If you’re deeply interested in Buddhism or local culture, it’s worth a visit. But for more casual visitors, it might not be very engaging without background knowledge.

In summary, Asgiri Maha Viharaya is a quiet and genuine place with cultural value, but it could be improved in terms of accessibility for international visitors. Still, I appreciated the chance to see a different side of Kandy and experience a more spiritual environment.

description: Museum in the former royal palace displaying weapons, artifacts & more from the Kandian era.

The Kandy National Museum is definitely worth visiting if you’re keen on the history and traditions of the Kandyan era. It houses a fascinating range of exhibits, from ancient weapons and ceremonial objects to traditional Kandyan attire and finely crafted items that showcase the artistry of the period.

The displays are thoughtfully arranged, with clear explanations that bring context to each artifact. Overall, the museum provides a rich insight into the heritage, culture, and craftsmanship of the Kandyan kingdom, along with glimpses of Sri Lanka’s colonial past.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kandy, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
St. Paul's Church, Milagiriya iss truly a place that offers both spiritual solace and historical richness. It is located in the heart of Bambalapitiya, Colombo, this Anglican Church has been a significant part of the local community for over 130 years. The serene atmosphere here makes it not only a place of worship but also a spot for peaceful reflection.

Originally established by the Portuguese as a Roman Catholic church, it was called "Nossa Senhora dos Milagres," which translates to "Our Lady of Miracles." The name Milagiriya is derived from the Portuguese word "Milagres," referring to miracles, and it’s said that a well near the original church was known for its supposed healing properties. People from across Sri Lanka would come here seeking spiritual and physical healing, which adds a layer of intrigue and reverence to the site.

The church today is part of the Diocese of Colombo and serves a diverse congregation from various walks of life, all united by their Christian faith. Whether you're a local or a visitor, there’s a real sense of belonging when you step inside. The church not only provides a space for worship but also plays an active role in guiding its members with Christian values.

Kandyan era paintings can be found inside the shrine room , the horizontal panels are quite larger than other temples, the reclining buddha statue belongs to southern style  ,  coner 2 statues are kandyan, cave is narrow and long and this temple contains the longest depiction of Màra paràjaya throughout the ceiling. description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kandy, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Adahanamaluwa gedige temple which is also known as Asgiriya temple/Asgiriya gedige, is a Buddhist monastery located in Asgiriya in the World Heritage city of Kandy in the Central province of Sri Lanka.

It is the headquarters of the Asgiriya chapter of the Siyam Nikaya, one of two Buddhist monasteries which holds the custodianship of the Sacred Tooth relic of the Sri Dalada Maligawa Temple.

The Asgiri Maha Viharaya traces it's origin back to the Wanawasi sect of the Dimbulagala Forest Monastery of the Polonnaruwa era.

This temple was founded in the era of King Parakramabahu IV (1305-1335 A.D.) and is believed to be considerably older than the Malwathu Maha Viharaya which is the other monastic centre of the Siyam Nikaya.

Asgiriya temple houses within it's premises the historically significant 'Adahana maluwa' or the Royal Cremation Ground of the Kandyan kingdom where the members of royal families were cremated and remains buried.

Historically, this gedige structure with it's unique ancient architectural features is the second oldest building in Kandy city.

Located in the hub of the Kandy city, it's a few minutes walking distance from the Temple of the Tooth relic.

Must visit for the history lover when in Kandy, Sri Lanka.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kandy, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
The Kidnapping of Sita by Ravana
Ravana was the king of Lanka and another 9 kingdoms. He was known as Dasis (or Dasa Shirsha) meaning 10 heads, because he had ten crowns, one each for his ten kingdoms.

His sister Surpanakha went to Jambudweepa for some business. Surpanakha chanced upon Rama at his hermitage and became enamored with the handsome prince. Rama being faithful to his wife, Sita, did not respond and asked Surpanakha to approach Lakshmana who was unmarried. Surpanakha, who felt humiliated by this, tried to attack Sita in anger saying Sita was the cause of the men’s contempt for her. Lakshmana then intervened and cut off Surpanakha’s nose.

Surpanakha, terrified and in pain, flew at once to Lanka to seek the protection of Ravana. When Ravana asked his sister for the cause, Surpanakha said that she had seen Sita, a lady of incomparable beauty, and wanted to bring her for Ravana. Ravana resolved to take revenge for the insult his sister had suffered, as well as to get lovely Sita for him self, and set out to abduct Sita and bring her to Lanka.

Ravana, using a golden deer as a decoy, visited Sita when she was alone. In the guise of an old sage, he abducted and brought her to Weragantota in Lanka in his plane, the Pushpaka vimana.

Weragantota means the "place of aircraft landing" in Sinhala. This is the first place Sita Devi was brought to Lankapura (capita city of king Ravana). These jungles are the place where the city of Lankapura once stood. The city had a beautiful palace for Queen Mandothari surrounded by waterfalls, streams and varieties of flora and fauna. Sita Devi was kept at Queen Mandothari’s palace at Lankapura. The place Sita was held captive is called Sita Kotuwa, which means "Sita’s Fort" in Sinhala. It is believed Ravana had an aircraft repair center at Gurulupotha close to Sita Kotuwa. Sita devi was kept in queen Mandothari’s palace until she was moved to Sita Kotuwa and then on to Ashoka Vatika. The remains that are found here are the remnants of later civilizations. In Valmiki's depiction, King Ravana’s vimana resembled a huge peacock. The vimana in Sinhala language means "Dhandu Monara" which is known as "flying peacock," and hence the name Gurulupotha, which means "parts of birds" in Sinhala. This is also called Gavagala.

The rose garden and orchid house are highlights when they are in bloom. The location under Hakgala Rock gives great views across the valley. There is a cafeteria where you can buy food and drinks, and small shops that sell flower plants and souvenirs.

A few areas could use better maintenance and clearer signs. Overall, it is worth visiting if you enjoy nature and photography. One of the best botanical gardens in Sri Lanka.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Nuwara Eliya, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Ambagamuwa inscriptions (epigraphs) are considered as one of the main sources of information in Polonnaruwa Era.

According to chronicles the king rested here en route to Sri Pada with his retinue while on pilgrimage.  The inscription has been made on the 36th year of King Vijayabahu I (1070-1110). These are engraved on 2 rocks which are 12 feet 3 inches x 9 feet 3 inches and the the other 9 feet 4 inches x 9 feet 7 inches in size.

These inscriptions describe the defeat of the Tamil invading forces by the King Vijayabahu I and bringing country under one rule, it also describes the work done by the king to the people and donations made to the Sri Pada pilgrims and the Sri Pada.

This inscription is located ab0out 400 meters from the Nawalapitiya – Ginigathhena Road (B319) at a vilage called Sellipigama in the Ambagamuwa town.

Unfortunately there is no archeology department board at the turn off to Sellipigama on the Nawalapitiya road. Either its broken down or never installed. The only indication is a cemented notice board on the ground at the top of the tiny road. This notice lies on the left of the road so that it is visible only if you are traveling from Nawapitiya.

This is a small tared road with most of the tar washed away. Travel about 400 meters along this road. This road will folk in to 3 small roads at about 200 meters and you need to take the left most road.

The inscriptions lies on a small triangular piece of land fenced by the archeological department. A small gate provides access into the rocks with inscriptions. Unfortunately this does not seem to be maintained properly with overgrown weeds surrounding the rocks. There is an open crack on one stone inscription which villagers say may be caused by the spreading roots of a breadfruit tree nearby.

Adams banglow haputale
It appears you may be referring to Adisham Bungalow in Haputale, as there is no prominent attraction named "Adams Bungalow" in the area, but "Adisham" is often misheard or misremembered.

The Adisham Bungalow, also known as Adisham Hall or St. Benedict's Monastery, is a significant colonial-era attraction near Haputale, Sri Lanka.

Here is a summary of the information:

🏰 Adisham Bungalow Overview
History: It was built in 1931 by Sir Thomas Lister Villiers, a prominent English planter and aristocrat, in the Tudor and Jacobean architectural styles, inspired by Leeds Castle in Kent.

Current Use: After Sir Thomas's retirement, the property changed hands and was eventually acquired by the Roman Catholic Church in 1961. It currently houses the Adisham Monastery of Saint Benedict.

Location: It is situated amidst the misty hills and tea plantations, about 3-4 km from Haputale town, offering panoramic views of the Uva valley.

Visitor Access: Parts of the house, including the library and living room, are open to the public on specific days, typically weekends, Poya days (Full Moon days), and public/school holidays, usually from 9:00 AM to 4:30 PM.

🌳 What to See and Do
Architecture: Admire the well-preserved Tudor and Jacobean style architecture, antique furniture, and period fittings.

Gardens: Stroll through the vast, enchanting English-style gardens, which are beautifully maintained.

Monastery Kiosk: A small shop on the premises sells homemade jams, pickles, and coffee liqueurs, often made with produce from the monastery farms.

Views: Enjoy the serene atmosphere and breathtaking views of the surrounding hills and valleys, including the mountain range known as the "Sleeping Warrior."

🛌 Accommodation
While the main Adisham Bungalow is an active monastery and not a typical commercial hotel, some search results indicate:

Limited Retreat Accommodation: The monastery sometimes offers limited accommodation for guests seeking a Christian spiritual retreat in an atmosphere of peace and solitude. Booking this usually requires contacting the monastery directly, often through the Father in charge, sometimes with a letter of recommendation.

Nearby Options: There are several other holiday bungalows and guesthouses in the vicinity of Haputale, such as "Adisham Village Home," which offer standard accommodation for travelers visiting the area.

Would you like me to look for the official contact information for Adisham Monastery for retreat booking inquiries, or would you be interested in finding alternative accommodation options near the Adisham Bungalow in Haputale?

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Matale, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
This Proto-historic Megalithic Burial Site, located near Dambulla, Sri Lanka, is a fascinating and historically rich archaeological site dating back over 2,700 years. This site provides remarkable insight into the lives, rituals, and burial practices of Sri Lanka’s early Iron Age communities. Thoughtfully maintained and well-signposted, the site offers an educational experience that is both immersive and reflective.

The burial ground, showcasing distinct chamber tombs made with stone slabs, reveals advanced burial techniques and social organization. The accompanying signage gives clear explanations in Sinhala, Tamil, and English, highlighting that over 42 burial sites have been excavated. Notably, the site distinguishes between urn burials and non-urn burials—some containing human remains along with grave goods like pottery, beads, and iron tools. This suggests the community had well-developed trade links and a structured society.

Visitors are required to purchase an entrance ticket, which is reasonably priced. Ample parking is available on-site, making it convenient even for group or family visits. The site is clean, quiet, and well-organized, with maintained pathways and shaded resting areas. One of the highlights is the availability of knowledgeable guides stationed at the site who are eager to explain the significance and history of the tombs. Their insights greatly enrich the experience, particularly for those unfamiliar with proto-historic South Asian archaeology.

The open-air layout and the rustic structures over the excavation areas provide a real sense of being close to the past, as you view the burial chambers exactly where they were discovered. Whether you are a history enthusiast, a student, or a curious traveler, Ibbankatuwa is a must-visit cultural site that offers a window into an ancient civilization hidden in plain sight.

A Hidden Gem – Arankele Buddhist Monastery Archaeological Site

Arankele is an incredible hidden treasure, showcasing unbelievable ancient stonework and architectural brilliance. This Buddhist monastery archaeological site is rich in history, featuring intricately carved pathways, meditation platforms, and ancient ruins that reflect the craftsmanship of centuries past.

description: Mountainous area centered on the Knuckles massif, known for waterfalls, hiking trails & birdlife.
display_review:
The Knuckles Mountain Range also called the Knuckles Massif; a bio-diversity hotspot located in the districts of Kandy and Matale in the Central Province of Sri Lanka. Situated at a height of over 3000 ft above sea level, covering a land area of approximately 21 ha it consists of five peaks; namely Kirigalpottha, Gombaniya, Knuckles, Koboneelagala and Dotulugala which form the shape of the knuckles in a clenched fist. The area situated above 1500 m was declared a climatic reserve in 1873 and a conservation forest in 2000, later a National Man & Biosphere Reserve and a World Heritage Site in 2009. It is today considered as one of South Asia’s important sites for the conservation of montane tropical forest habitats.

This picturesque mist laden mountain range is where the Knuckles Forest Reserve also known as the Knuckles Conservation forest is found. It is an UNESCO designated World Heritage Site that comes under the Central Highlands of Sri Lanka’s World Heritage Property, one of the two World Heritage Natural Properties in Sri Lanka.

The climate here varies from hot to very wet and cold with some higher elevations reporting ground frost during January – March with strong winds at most other times. The vegetation in the valleys and foothills consist mainly of semi evergreen vegetation with large trees, the upper slopes contain tropical montane humid evergreen rain forest and the upper levels possess cloud forests; a moist forest with a low-level cloud cover where various endemic species of ferns, orchids, lichens and mosses grow profusely. The other types of unique vegetation found here includes the distinctively beautiful grasslands called “Pitawala Patana”, the picturesque Riverine forests alongside the rivers and waterways, the Scrublands and the Pygmy forests with its twisted and gnarled plant life that grow up to 1-2 m from ground level.

The Knuckles massif separates itself from the central hills by the Dumbara Valley; a deep gorge. There are several trails to the Knuckles Mountain range, each one different and exclusive, making it ideal for hiking, trekking, waterfall hunting, bird watching and certainly a must place for some scenic photography. Visitors can travel on three routes from Kandy via Rattota, via Wattegama and via Teldeniya.

The Knuckles Forest reserve is considered a vital water catchment with many of its streams feeding important downstream rivers such as the gigantic Mahaweli River (the country’s longest), the Heen River, Maha Oya, Hasalaka Oya and the Theligamu Oya.

The Forest Reserve has recorded up of 1033 plant species belonging to 141 families of which 15% are endemic. In addition there are 128 bird species of which 17 are endemic; some of them are the Black eagle, Pale billed flower pecker, Barbets, Lorikeets, Egrets, Herons, Bulbuls, Babblers, Kingfishers and Wood peckers. The 31 mammal species include the Wild boar, Black-naped hare, Mouse deer, Sambar deer, Elephant, Wild buffalo, Monkey, Squirrel, Civet cat, Golden palm cat, Loris, Jackal, Mongoose, Bandicoot and Porcupine. The 20 amphibians’ species include the Kirthisingha’s Rock Frog and the Leaf nosed lizard, 15 species of fish include the Phillips Garra, Blotched filamented barb and Martenstyn barb. There are 60 species of butterflies, with two endemic species the Birdwing and the Blue Mormon. There are also 17 species of mollusks and 53 species of reptiles.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Matale, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
This magnificent waterfall is situated close to Rattota town in Matale District. Take Matale – Ilukumbura road to the Bamarakiri Falls bend, which is 1km past Rattota town, where the fall is to be found. The Bambarakiri Falls is 18km from Matale town. The Bambarakiri Falls is located approximately 40km away from our Amaya Hunas Falls, one of the most popular honeymoon hotels in Sri Lanka.

The fall is set amidst the mesmerizing greenery of the Knuckles mountain range. Bambarakiri Ella is a fall of 10m. The fall can be viewed in three parts. Different varieties of butterflies and birds come to the area of the falls mostly in clear mornings making the view even more charming.

A very old-fashioned suspension bridge extends over the falls adding an enchanting touch to the already beautiful scene.

Gallons of water come crashing down to earth at a high speed. The rock pool at the base of the waterfall is safe for a dip during the dry summer, but can be very dangerous during heavy rainy season.

For those who yearn for more adventure, there is a steep slippery climb of 5km, to reach the top of the waterfall. But one should bear in mind that a slight mistake will result in grave injury.

Although the climb is not easy and should be done with care, the views of the landscape from the top are breathtaking. In addition, there is a wonderful bonus for those that make the effort of the climb – a cool and crystal clear natural rock pool perfect for bathing.

This tranquil and peaceful place is indeed a hidden gem in Matale. Visitors who come to the areas in Matale for holidays are strongly recommended to visit this beautiful place.

How To Reach Bambarakiri Ella
Bambarakiri Falls are located approximately 200 m away from a bend of the Matale-Pallegala-Hasalaka mountain road and almost 5 Kms uphill from Rattota town. From the Matale city, the distance is nearly 16 Kms.

One can also book private cabs to Matale or Kandy and finally towards the Rattota town and then a nature trail towards the fall.

Public transport towards Matale and Kandy is also available.

We visited the Dutch Hospital Precinct with my wife and our 10-year-old child, and it was one of the most charming spots in Colombo! The colonial architecture is beautifully preserved, and the evening atmosphere was lively yet relaxing.

We had a wonderful meal at Ministry of Crab, which truly lived up to its reputation, and did some souvenir shopping at I ❤️ Sri Lanka. The area is compact but full of character — perfect for a leisurely walk, dining, and browsing local shops.

It was conveniently located right beside our hotel, and we loved watching the pigeons at the front entrance — a small but lovely touch that made the place feel alive.

description: Longstanding tower featuring a large clock face, built in 1883 on top of a former guard room.
display_review:
Standing tall at the entrance of the historic Galle Fort, the Galle Clock Tower is one of those landmarks that immediately grabs your attention and stays in your memory long after you leave. On my visit, I was struck not just by its imposing structure but also by the history it carries within its walls. Built in the 19th century during the British colonial era, the tower feels like a timeless guardian, overlooking the fort and the vibrant city beyond.
What makes the experience special is the setting. Surrounded by the ramparts of Galle Fort and lush greenery, the clock tower offers a stunning vantage point. From here, you can look out over the Indian Ocean, with waves crashing against the rocks below—a breathtaking contrast of stone and sea. It’s the perfect spot for photography, whether you want to capture the tower itself or the panoramic views stretching in every direction.
I enjoyed strolling around the area, soaking in the atmosphere of the fort, and then pausing by the tower to simply admire how seamlessly history and nature blend here. It’s also a great meeting point, as many locals and visitors alike gather around the tower before exploring the rest of the fort.
What adds to the charm is the sense of calm despite being in a popular destination. Standing under the tower, you can hear the ocean breeze and the distant chatter of people enjoying their day. It’s peaceful, yet alive with stories.
For anyone visiting Galle, the Clock Tower is more than just a quick photo stop—it’s a chance to feel connected to the heritage of the city. A simple yet powerful symbol of Galle’s colonial past, it’s definitely worth the visit.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Galle, Sri Lanka.
display_review: Only built in 1904, Meeran Mosque exhibits a fabulous mix of architectural styles that again showcases the amazing diversity of cultures that have lived in and ruled over Galle throughout the years. While its Baroque facade looks more like that of a cathedral, some incredible Islamic detailing and decorations remind us that we are indeed looking at a mosque. Add in various British Victorian features, Dutch-influenced designs, and patterned Italian floor tiles, and you start to get a snapshot of not only the history and architecture of the mosque but that of Galle as well. The elegant whitewashed building certainly stands out against its surroundings. It is this unusual and unique combination that makes Meeran Mosque so worth visiting.

description: Simple home of a Sri Lankan author with displays on Buddhist culture, set in tree-dotted grounds.

A must-visit for anyone interested in the authentic heart of Sri Lankan culture. It's also a surprisingly great spot for families—the kids in our group were completely fascinated by the old bullock carts and horse-drawn carriages on display.

Beyond the fascinating artifacts, the true magic is in the setting. The museum is surrounded by lush, serene gardens and the natural beauty of Koggala, making the entire experience incredibly calm and peaceful. It’s the perfect place to wander, reflect, and enjoy the tranquility. All in all, it’s a wonderful, insightful, and highly recommended experience for visitors of all ages looking to connect with the island's past amidst beautiful nature

This more than life size relief carved in solid rock is thought to be one of the best statues in the Mahayanist Buddhism in Sri Lanka. Special features:

The shrine is located on the coast, with the original shrine situated on a small island in the sea.

The original shrine can only be reached by boat.

It has a long history of religious and folk beliefs.

Devol Maduwa is a well-known ritual performance held here:

Mostly takes place during May–June.

Involves traditional dances, songs, and religious rituals to drive away evil spirits and seek blessings.

Ceremonies often continue throughout the night, blending religious devotion with music and dance.

description: Laid-back hotel in a Dutch Colonial building offering sea views, plus an airy restaurant & a bar.
display_review:
Review: Recent Stay at Closenberg Hotel

Staff:
The staff are extremely kind, warm, and genuinely helpful. Their friendliness truly stands out. With that said, some additional training—especially in telephone etiquette and professional communication—would greatly enhance the overall guest experience.

Room & Bed:
The rooms have a charming vintage character, and the bed is super comfortable, ensuring a great night’s sleep. However, housekeeping needs closer attention, particularly proper dusting and overall cleanliness. While a dental kit is not provided, it’s not a major concern as this seems to align with the hotel’s concept.

Bathroom:
The bathroom is an area that requires improvement. The bidet feels outdated; a bidet shower would be more practical. A reliable hot water system with good water pressure is essential and should be addressed.

View & Balcony:
The view from the balcony is absolutely unbeatable—one of the best I’ve experienced. For better privacy, it would be great if balconies could be partially screened, as neighboring balconies are visible.

Pool:
The infinity-style pool (the type that visually merges with the ocean) is located in a truly unique, one-of-a-kind spot with breathtaking views. This area has huge untapped potential. A pool bar, ambient lighting, bean bags, music, Wi-Fi, and drinks could transform it into a major Instagrammable attraction and a strong revenue generator for the hotel.

Food:
The food is amazing and absolutely delicious—clearly one of the hotel’s strongest points.

Atmosphere & Maintenance:
Despite its charm and potential, basic maintenance has been seriously neglected. Issues noticed include lack of garden grooming, food placed on the floor in hallways for cats, a generally run-down appearance, broken gutters, and water leaking during rain—even inside the hotel, with containers placed to collect water. A broken stairway leading to the ocean is overgrown with weeds. These are fundamental issues that need urgent attention.

Restaurant & Coral Displays:
Large coral installations inside the restaurant take up significant space and appear old, poorly maintained, and neglected, with dried plants and unkept fish tanks. Similar coral displays were seen in other areas. It would be far better if these were relocated back to the ocean, allowing the restaurant space to breathe and function as intended.

Landscaping:
The path to the pool is surrounded by greenery, but much of it feels unplanned. The hotel would benefit greatly from a proper horticultural redesign.

Overall:
This hotel is ideal for honeymooners due to its limited number of rooms and romantic setting, but more work is needed before it can fully cater to a niche market. Personally, I love this hotel—it offers one of the best views I’ve ever experienced. It’s truly sad to see such an iconic property slowly losing its charm due to neglect rather than lack of potential.

I sincerely hope management takes serious note of these points and works toward restoring Closenberg Hotel to its former glory.

display_review:
Thawalama Ambalama.

Located at the Batahena junction in the Batahena Grama Niladhari Division of the Thawalama Divisional Secretariat Division of the Hiniduma Patthuwa Pradeshiya Sabha in the Galle District, the historical background of the Thawalama town is well understood. This Ambalama was built to pay off the hard work of the caravans who used to carry goods on carts and trade in the past. The goods transported along the Ganga were loaded onto the carts from this Ambalama. Mr. Dong Koranelis Wijesekara built this Ambalama in 2458 AD. This Ambalama, which has a small porch and a room, has a suitable seat installed. The roof is covered with local tiles, and the Ministry of Culture carried out renovation work on this ambalam in 2018.

During our stay in Matara, we visited the Star Fort, a fascinating historical site originally built by the Dutch during their colonial period. It was later used by the British and subsequently by the Sri Lankan government. Since 2009, the fort has been open to the public as a museum, showcasing the rich history, cultural heritage and significant landmarks of Matara.

The museum also features a souvenir shop offering beautifully illustrated booklets on Sri Lankan heritage at very reasonable prices. I purchased two of them; one on Sri Lankan art and another on art preservation, both filled with valuable information and colorful photographs.

A special mention goes to Lahiru, a staff member from the Department of Archaeology, who warmly guided us through the exhibits with detailed explanations. His attentiveness and hospitality made our visit even more memorable.

No prior reservation is required, and the entrance fee is just Rs. 100 per person. We visited on a Sunday evening and truly enjoyed this enriching experience.

description: A historical fortification in Matara, serving as a testament to the colonial history of Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Tharaka Kotuwa, also known as the Star Fort, is a small Dutch-built fortress located on the eastern bank of the Nilwala River in Matara, Sri Lanka. It was constructed between 1763 and 1765 by the Dutch, soon after they regained control of Matara following the Matara Rebellion of 1761, when the local forces captured the main fort.

To strengthen the city’s inland defenses, the Dutch Governor Van Eck ordered the building of this new fort, which was named Redoute Van Eck in his honour. The fort has a six-pointed star shape, designed to fire cannons in all directions, and is surrounded by a moat. Its thick granite and coral walls, two prison cells, central well, and drainage system showcase excellent 18th-century military engineering.

When the British took over Ceylon in 1796, the fort lost its military importance and was used for administrative work. Later, it became a public library and was then taken over by the Department of Archaeology. Major restoration work was done in the 1980s, and today the fort is preserved as a historical monument and museum.

Standing as one of the last forts built by the Dutch in Sri Lanka, Tharaka Kotuwa remains a proud symbol of Matara’s colonial heritage and architectural beauty.

If you’re visiting Waligama and looking for a serene place to reflect, worship, or learn about Buddhist culture, Agrabodi Raja Maha Viharaya is truly worth a visit. A peaceful, meaningful, and spiritually uplifting experience.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Matara, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
⚠️ This is the  Forgotten Treasure in Matara ⚠️

The Dutch Market at Nupe Junction, Matara is one of the most unique surviving monuments of Dutch architecture in Sri Lanka, but sadly it suffers from poor conservation and neglect. Built with remarkable craftsmanship, it features a well designed in Dutch style, distinctive roofing with unusual tiles (උළු) different from other Dutch structures, and even wooden carvings along the roof edges. A charming garden still survives, and the premises also house an archaeological office and bookshop. Yet, despite its historic value, the site is largely treated as an exhibition hall, where exhibitors are unaware of its importance and may unknowingly cause damage. The Archaeological Department has placed a signboard here, but has done little to actively protect or preserve the monument.

Getting here is very easy — it’s just a few minutes from the Matara Bus Stand and Matara Railway Station, and sits right at Nupe Junction, with Pizza Hut opposite and Keells supermarket nearby. This makes it a perfect stop for travelers to explore quickly while enjoying a rest, snack, or some shopping in the area.

However, the people of Matara must be more responsible in preserving this landmark. It is not just an old building, but a rare piece of history that reflects the city’s colonial past. Travelers should be informed of its importance, and the authorities should take stronger steps to conserve and showcase this heritage site. Without urgent attention, Matara risks losing one of its most unique Dutch-era treasures forever.

description: 18th-century temple depicting the punishments of hell, plus statues & murals of the Buddha's life.
display_review:
Wewurukannala Vihara in Dikwella is one of the most interesting and unforgettable temples in southern Sri Lanka. The biggest attraction here is the huge seated Buddha statue, which is about 160 feet tall. It is one of the tallest Buddha statues in the country and looks very impressive when you stand in front of it.

Before reaching the statue, you have to walk through the famous “Tunnel of Hell.” This tunnel is dark and full of large statues and paintings that show punishments for people who do bad things. It can look scary and strange, but it is made to teach people to live a good and kind life. Each scene gives a lesson about the results of bad behavior such as lying, stealing, or hurting others.

When you come out of the tunnel and see the tall, peaceful Buddha statue, it feels very meaningful. The change from the dark tunnel to the bright open space gives a strong message about moving from sin and fear to peace and understanding.

Around the temple, there are also smaller shrines, colorful wall paintings, and quiet places to rest. Wewurukannala Vihara is not just a temple — it is a special place that mixes religion, art, and moral lessons. It gives visitors an unforgettable experience and a lot to think about.

Visiting during the early morning or evening adds an even more magical feeling, with the soft light enhancing its pure white glow. The devotees’ faith and the sound of temple bells create a sense of connection that’s hard to describe — it’s something you must experience for yourself.

A must-visit sacred site for anyone exploring Sri Lanka’s cultural heritage or seeking spiritual peace. 🌼🙏

Visited Ridiyagama Safari Park with my family and had a really great experience. We got to see lions, elephants, giraffes, zebras, and so many other animals across different zones, and the guides explained a lot of interesting facts along the way.

description: An ancient irrigation reservoir in Hambantota, built by kings to support local agriculture.
display_review:
Long ago, after the fall of the great city of Anuradhapura, a new capital rose from the heart of Sri Lanka’s dry plains — Polonnaruwa, the second royal city of the Sinhalese kingdom. Between the 10th and 12th centuries, this city flourished under visionary rulers like King Vijayabahu I, who freed the island from South Indian Chola invaders, and King Parakramabahu I, whose reign ushered in a golden age of art, architecture, and irrigation. Today, the Polonnaruwa Ancient City, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, stands as a living museum of that brilliance — a place where time seems to have paused in reverence to history.

As you enter the archaeological park, you’ll feel like you’ve stepped into another era. The Royal Palace complex still whispers of royal banquets and grand councils, while the Sacred Quadrangle stuns visitors with its intricate shrines and ornate stone carvings. A short ride away, the Gal Vihara takes your breath away — four colossal Buddha statues carved directly into granite, each radiating serenity that transcends centuries. Polonnaruwa’s layout is compact and well-organized, making it ideal for exploring by bicycle, gliding quietly past lotus ponds, moonstones, and towering stupas that gleam in the tropical sun.

Visiting Polonnaruwa is not just a historical excursion — it’s an experience steeped in mindfulness and respect. Modest dress is expected, and shoes must be removed before entering sacred spaces, so carrying socks can help when the stone ground heats up under the midday sun. Arriving early in the morning, between 8 and 9 a.m., rewards travelers with cooler air and a sense of tranquility before the crowds arrive.

Whether you come from Dambulla, Kandy, or Colombo, the journey is well worth it. For a steep $30 USD, you gain entry to the vast ruins, the on-site museum, which provides fascinating context to what you’ll see among the stones. The best months to visit are December to March, when the skies are dry and clear — perfect for pedaling through history.

In Polonnaruwa, every carving and column tells a story — of devotion, innovation, and timeless beauty. It is smaller and easier to navigate than Anuradhapura, yet no less grand in spirit. By the time you stand before the serene Buddhas of Gal Vihara, you’ll understand why this city remains one of Sri Lanka’s most unforgettable windows into its ancient soul.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Hambantota, Sri Lanka.
A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Hambantota, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Ussangoda National Park is one of Sri Lanka's most unique coastal destinations, perfect for visitors seeking a peaceful escape with dramatic scenery.

• The Red Soil: The park's most famous feature is its vast, open plateau of striking red soil. This unique, brick-coloured landscape gives the area an almost "Martian" feel, especially against the blue sky. Local legends and geological theories debate its origin—some say it’s from a meteor impact, while others point to high mineral concentrations. Walking across this red earth is an unforgettable experience.

• The Beautiful Beach: A trail through the park leads you to the edge of high cliffs. From here, you get breathtaking, panoramic views of the Indian Ocean.

• A Good Place to Relax: Because it is less crowded than other parks, Ussangoda is an excellent place to relax. The quiet atmosphere, the sound of the wind, and the stunning views of the red land meeting the blue sea make it an incredibly peaceful spot. It’s perfect for a quiet walk, photography, or simply sitting on the cliffs and enjoying the serene environment.
In short, Ussangoda is a hidden gem that offers a perfect blend of unique geology, coastal beauty, and pure relaxation.

place_name: Great Basses and Little Basses Reefs
description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Hambantota, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
I Visiting the Great Basses Reef Lighthouse was one of the most unique and unforgettable experiences I’ve had in Sri Lanka. This historic offshore lighthouse, standing 13 km into the ocean near Yala National Park, is absolutely breathtaking and has so much history behind it. The boat ride itself was an adventure, passing through the deep blue waters until the lighthouse appeared on the reef.

I arranged the trip through Malima Diving Center Kirinda and with the support of the Sri Lanka Coast Guard Station in Kirinda, who made the journey safe and well organized. Their service was excellent, and they guided us carefully all the way.

The sight of the Great Basses Lighthouse rising from the reef is truly spectacular, and being out there in the middle of the sea feels magical. It’s not a common tourist spot since it’s only accessible by boat, but that makes it even more special. If you love history, marine adventures, or just want a one-of-a-kind travel memory, this is highly recommended.

Visiting Maligathanna Raja Maha Viharaya was a peaceful and rewarding experience. Tucked away in the serene hills, the temple offers a calm, spiritual atmosphere and a glimpse into Sri Lanka’s rich Buddhist heritage. The hike to the top was well worth it—not just for the cultural significance of the site, but for the breathtaking panoramic view that unfolds once you reach the summit. The best time to visit is early in the morning or late afternoon when the weather is cooler and the soft light enhances the beauty of the surroundings. Sunrise and sunset, in particular, create a magical ambiance at the top, making the whole experience unforgettable.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Jaffna, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
Sacred Springs with a Serene Atmosphere 💧

The Keerimalai Wells (also known as the Keerimalai Freshwater Springs) are a peaceful and sacred natural site located just north of Jaffna, right next to the Naguleswaram Shiva Temple and the coastline. These springs are believed to have healing properties, and for centuries, locals have bathed here as part of religious and cultural traditions.

The water is crystal clear and naturally fed, and it’s fascinating that the springs are right by the ocean, yet remain fresh due to an underground source. The separation of men's and women’s bathing areas allows for privacy, and there’s a quiet, spiritual vibe to the entire place — especially if you go early in the morning or on a weekday.

🌿 What to Expect:

The area is modest and traditional — don't expect resort-style pools, but rather natural, open-air baths used primarily by locals
Many people visit for ritual cleansing or spiritual reasons, especially before entering the nearby temple
The surrounding area is scenic, with sea views and palm trees adding to the charm
It’s best to dress modestly and bring your own towel or change of clothes

⚠️ Note:

The site could benefit from better maintenance and cleanliness — it’s a bit basic in terms of facilities
Some areas may be slippery or aged, so walk with care
If you're interested in local traditions, natural healing sites, or quiet spiritual places, Keerimalai Springs are definitely worth a visit. It’s not a long stop, but a meaningful one — especially when combined with Naguleswaram Kovil next door.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Kilinochchi, Sri Lanka.
Sacred, historic temple said to be visited by the Buddha with paintings by a renowned artist.
display_review:
A Sacred Treasure of Sri Lankan Buddhism
The Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara is one of Sri Lanka's most ancient and revered Buddhist temples, constantly filled with devoted worshippers and curious visitors from around the world. This sacred site is not just a place of worship—it's a living masterpiece of art and history.
What makes this temple truly extraordinary are the stunning murals and hand-painted artworks that adorn its walls. These paintings showcase the unique style of Sri Lankan Buddhist art, created by master artists whose work has drawn admirers from across the globe. The intricate detail, vibrant colors, and spiritual storytelling in these murals are unlike anything you'll find elsewhere. Art enthusiasts and historians travel from far and wide specifically to witness these remarkable creations.
The temple grounds are always bustling with activity, but there's something magical about visiting in the evening. As the sun sets, the temple comes alive with a serene beauty—the soft lighting, the sound of evening prayers, and the peaceful atmosphere create an unforgettable experience.
What makes Kelaniya Raja Maha Vihara especially sacred is that it houses precious relics of Lord Buddha, enshrined within the temple's pagoda (dagoba). According to tradition, the Buddha himself visited this site during his third visit to Sri Lanka, making it one of the most significant pilgrimage sites for Buddhists.

Practical Tips for Visitors:
Don't forget to buy flowers to offer to the Buddha—there are plenty of flower vendors available near the parking area. The temple has ample parking space, but it's important to note: do not leave your valuables in the vehicle. It's highly recommended to carry your belongings with you or have someone stay with the vehicle for security.
Whether you're a devotee seeking spiritual solace or a traveler interested in culture and history, this temple offers a profound and beautiful experience. Highly recommended for anyone visiting Sri Lanka!

description: A historical fortification in Mannar, serving as a testament to the colonial history of Sri Lanka.
display_review:
A Testament to Resilience, Waiting to Tell Its Full Story
​The Mannar Dutch Fort is a truly captivating historical site, offering a raw and authentic glimpse into Sri Lanka's colonial past. It's not a polished, museum-like experience; rather, it's a magnificent, albeit partially ruined, structure that beautifully captures the essence of a bygone era.

​Architecture and Atmosphere:

The fort's distinctive four-bastioned, square design is immediately impressive, a signature of Dutch military engineering. Its massive coral-stone walls are what truly set it apart. Walking along the ramparts, you can feel the sheer scale of the construction.

What makes it unique is the quiet solitude here—it lacks the heavy crowds of more southern forts, allowing for a much more personal and reflective experience as you imagine the centuries of history that unfolded within these walls, from Portuguese to Dutch to British control.

​The Current State and Experience:

Currently, the fort is in a state of partial ruin, which is part of its charm. You can see the layers of history—the thick outer walls, the remnants of internal structures, and the quiet grounds now often grazed by local deer.

While there isn't much in terms of explanatory signage, its value lies in its atmosphere. It's an excellent location for photographers who appreciate the texture of old stone and dramatic, weathered settings. The views from the top of the ramparts offer a fantastic perspective of the surrounding area, including a glimpse of the nearby causeway and the coastal landscape.

​Historical Context:

Originally built by the Portuguese in the 16th century and later rebuilt by the Dutch in the 1650s, the fort has seen significant action. Its strategic location on Mannar Island, guarding the access point to the mainland, underscores its historical importance. For those interested in military history or colonial architecture, this is a profound place of pilgrimage.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Mannar, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
The baobab tree, often referred to as the bottle tree, upside-down tree, monkey bread tree, baobab, boab, and boaboa, stands as a testament to resilience and endurance. Native to Africa, Madagascar, and Australia, this remarkable tree boasts eight species, with six found in Madagascar and one each in Africa and Australia. In Sri Lanka, it is known as "Ali-Gaha," or "Elephant Tree," reflecting the unique beauty of its bark, which resembles elephant skin. The Tamil community refers to it as "Perukka."

Believed to have arrived in Sri Lanka through Arabian traders, the baobabs inspire awe. A 2003 study uncovered approximately 40 surviving trees, with 34 of them documented in Mannar, many of which have thrived for 300 to 400 years. The oldest and largest baobab, located in Pallimunei, embodies the spirit of longevity, estimated to be 800 years old, having previously been recorded at 723 years. In 1890, it measured 61 feet (18.6 meters) around and stood 30 feet (9.1 meters) tall. Today, it has a circumference of 19.5 meters and a height of 7.5 meters, remaining a symbol of strength and wisdom as captured in images taken in May 2008.

description: A prominent historical and cultural landmark located in Mannar, Sri Lanka.
display_review:
The Doric Bungalow in Arippu, Mannar is a historic old house built in the early 1800s for Frederick North, the first British Governor of Ceylon. It is called the “Doric” Bungalow because its pillars were made in the Doric style, one of the simple ancient Greek designs. The Governor used this place as his residence and to manage the pearl fishing work in the Mannar area. Today, the building is mostly in ruins because of sea erosion and weather, but it is still an important archaeological site by the beach.
This is a great place to visit and experience the colonial era while enjoying the fresh, salty sea breeze of the north-west coast. It is truly a must-visit spot when travelling in Mannar, and the government authorities should take action to protect these valuable sites for the future. 19.04.2025

🌊 Mannar had been known for its seas which were home to high quality pearls, the people who worked under Fredrick North and stayed at this fort would have been overseeing the pearl fisheries.

🏠 The Fort later become a guest house before being abandoned in the civil war.

👸 The fort also has Tamil legend linked to it.
